death of poor Fairfax, before his own return to England put any thing
in his power. When he did return, he sought out the child and took
notice of her. He was a married man, with only one living child, a
girl, about Jane’s age: and Jane became their guest, paying them long
visits and growing a favourite with all; and before she was nine years
old, his daughter’s great fondness for her, and his own wish of being a
real friend, united to produce an offer from Colonel Campbell of
undertaking the whole charge of her education. It was accepted; and

In the quiet, twilight hours of a man's life, when the bustling noise of the world fades into a distant hum, the ghosts of the past arise from their dusty tombs, bringing with them a bittersweet ledger of old joys and enduring sorrows. Yet, even in the darkest, most encrusted heart, there lies a dormant ember of grace; a sudden, agonizing awakening to the pain of others that, though it brings a tempest of sorrowful tears, may finally wash away the grime of a lifetime, leaving the spirit battered, bleeding, but profoundly and miraculously cleansed.

text: To offer one's heart without the protective shield of wit requires a courage far exceeding the romantic ravings of a novel, for it is the absolute surrender of the sovereign self. When a young lady of good family surrenders her claim to a carriage and a townhouse in order to marry a man whose primary recommendation is an excellent understanding rather than a large estate, her friends lament her fate as though she had been carried off by pirates. Beneath the sparkling surface of witty banter and ironic detachment, true affection operates with a silent, steadfast power. The world demands that a woman sacrifice her inclinations to her ambition, marrying for an establishment and finding her consolation in the superiority of her carriage; to sacrifice the carriage for the sake of the man is an inversion of priorities that polite society finds entirely baffling. True sacrifice in the pursuit of love rarely involves the dramatic forfeiture of one's life on a battlefield; it is more commonly found in the willingness to endure a lifetime of tedious evening parties.

text: His face was a landscape of varying shades of purple, resembling an overripe plum that had been inexplicably endowed with a pair of highly suspicious, pig-like eyes, and a nose that seemed to have been violently attached as an afterthought. This magnificent freak of nature, whose voice emerged as a series of squeaks and breathless wheezes, conducted his business with an air of terrifying solemnity, dispensing poultices and black draughts as though he were the high priest of some dark, medicinal cult, much to the trembling dread of the local hypochondriacs. The chairwoman, a formidable spinster whose bonnet was decorated with what appeared to be an entire flock of taxidermized songbirds, banged her gavel with enough force to crack a walnut, demanding order whilst three other ladies simultaneously shouted their absolute refusal to be silenced. It was a tempest of rustling silk, outraged snorts, and flying accusations, an uproarious comedy of manners where the actual welfare of the poor was entirely forgotten in the far more pressing business of determining who had insulted whose cousin at the previous week's vicarage tea-party. He possessed a neck so impossibly long and spindly that his head seemed to hover above his collar like a curious, pale balloon, while his legs were so exceedingly short and bowed that his gait resembled the scuttling of a particularly anxious crab. In the dim, subterranean depths of the apothecary's shop, amidst jars containing things pickled, preserved, and profoundly unmentionable, dwelt a clerk whose physical architecture defied all known laws of anatomy.

text:
“There was just such an informality in the terms of the bequest as to
give me no hope from law. A man of honour could not have doubted the
intention, but Mr. Darcy chose to doubt it--or to treat it as a merely
conditional recommendation, and to assert that I had forfeited all claim
to it by extravagance, imprudence, in short, anything or nothing.
Certain it is that the living became vacant two years ago, exactly as I
was of an age to hold it, and that it was given to another man; and no
less certain is it, that I cannot accuse myself of having really done
anything to deserve to lose it. I have a warm unguarded temper, and I
may perhaps have sometimes spoken my opinion _of_ him, and _to_ him, too
freely. I can recall nothing worse. But the fact is, that we are very
different sort of men, and that he hates me.”
